interactions.summary: The host asks Simon why oil equities and services are rising even as the commodity itself stays weak.
Simon says the divergence shows an asset-stripping regime where debt and financial engineering prop up corporations even while the underlying commodity or state balance sheet weakens.
As the conversation nears closing, Simon proposes two live scorecards for the debate: whether Al-Aqsa falls and whether the dollar keeps weakening.

claims.claim: Simon argues that stock prices no longer reflect earnings reality but instead reflect debt rollover, passive-index concentration, media management, and geopolitical importance to financial power.
Simon says the government hands power to corporations and corporations hand it onward to finance, with technical power then contesting the top of that hierarchy.
Simon proposes that the fate of Al-Aqsa Mosque and the long-term strength or weakness of the dollar are the two empirical benchmarks that will adjudicate the debate.

signature_moments.moment: The market stops being a price-discovery machine and becomes a managed derivative-political weapon.
signature_moments.source_phrase: there is no such thing as a stock price anymore
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is one of Simon's starkest market formulations and would likely carry into the public read as a memorable thesis line.

chronology_notes.note: Before formal closing statements, Simon explicitly turns the discussion into a forward-looking scorecard based on one geopolitical event and one monetary trend.

uncertainty_notes.note: The claims about market manipulation and subordination describe Simon's explanatory framework for the chart behavior; they are not direct proof of specific institutions executing each mechanism.
